subject: [RFC PATCH v2 0/4] mm/zsmalloc: reduce zs_free() latency on swap release path

reviews.author: Kairui Song
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Kairui Song noted that zs_free() acquires pool->lock while compaction writes-acquires the same lock, causing contention between compaction and zs free-ers, and requested a perf trace on process teardown to determine if async zs-freeing is necessary or if zsmalloc's poor scalability in the free path can be addressed.

Reviewer Kairui Song noted that freeing a swap entry also releases the backing compression data, which can lead to slower memory pressure reduction due to fragmentation and the need to free many swap entries for each folio.

Reviewer Kairui Song suggested implementing a bulk zs_free() operation to reduce the number of individual frees and potentially improve performance. They also expressed concerns about the potential drawbacks of asynchronous freeing, including increased worker overhead and delayed memory pressure.

Reviewer suggested optimizing zs_free() by using a percpu queue and attempting to free the whole queue under a read lock if successful
reviews.raw_body:
Thanks for adding me to the Cc list :), Barry started this idea with
ZRAM, which looks very interesting to me.

---

It's not about decompression. I think what Wenchao means here is that:
freeing the swap entry also releases the backing compression data, but
compared to freeing an actual folio (which bring back a free folio to
reduce memory pressure), you may need to free a lot of swap entries to
free one whole folio, because the compressed data could be much
smaller than folio and with fragmentation. And swap entry freeing is
still not fast enough to be ignored.

---

That's a very nice insight. I had an idea previously that can we have
something like a zs free bulk? Freeing handles one by one does seem
expensive.
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/adt3Q_SRToF6fb3W@KASONG-MC4/

It might be tricky to do so though.

It will be best if we can speed up everything, doing things async
doesn't reduce the total amount of work, and might cause more trouble
like worker overhead or delayed freeing causing more memory pressure,
if the workqueue didn't run in time. Or maybe a process is almost
completely swapped out, then this won't help at all.

I'm not against the async idea, they might combine well.

---

That's interesting, when I mentioned zs free bulk I was thinking that,
if we have a percpu queue, at least we may try read lock that on every
enqueue, free the whole queue if successful, then release the lock.
I'm sure there are more ways to optimize that, just a random idea :)

reviews.author: Xueyuan Chen
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Xueyuan Chen noted that the current implementation of zsmalloc free operations may not be optimized for contiguous ranges of swap slots to be freed, suggesting batching opportunities to reduce overhead.

The reviewer noted that free_zspage() acquires the class->lock while holding the zone->lock, creating a nested lock dependency and significantly extending the hold time of the class->lock, which causes other CPUs to wait longer.

2026-04-22.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function zs_free_deferred() for asynchronous handle freeing in zsmalloc. The original implementation of locking, grabbing a handle, and unlocking one at a time is inefficient and can be improved by using a workqueue or per-cpu-fying the batches/queues to reduce concurrency issues. An alternative approach suggested is to use two arrays of handles, swapping them under a lock when the defer worker is woken up, and then freeing the handles in the old array.
